evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's birth cohorts are in steep, accelerating decline: 77,500 children born in 2024 (lowest on record) and approximately 72,000 in 2025 (7.1% fewer than 2024), with total fertility rate fallen to 1.31 children per woman, the lowest in 14 years and far below replacement level of 2.1.

sources.title: KSH (Hungarian Central Statistical Office) 2024-2025 vital statistics

evidence_appendix.claim: Cohort decline is structurally embedded for at least 20+ years due to dual mechanism: declining total fertility rate per woman combined with shrinking pool of reproductive-age women, independent of short-term behavioral fluctuations.

sources.title: Hungarian demographic analysis of fertility and maternal cohort dynamics

evidence_appendix.claim: At baseline, approximately 40% of Hungarian primary schools have zero students attempting selective-track entrance exams (60% in village schools), while better schools in county seats and Budapest experience class-size drops of 20-30% by grade 7 as students leave for gimnázium, indicating selective tracks function as urban/regional-center phenomenon with minimal rural uptake.

sources.title: Berényi Eszter (Hungarian education sociologist) competence-test data analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: Rural settlements in Western Transdanubia, Southern Baranya, and low-density regions of Northern Hungary show child population contractions of 10-30% over recent periods, with some settlements reporting only 50-70 school-age children total, creating structural viability challenges for multi-track (4/6/8-year) selective pipelines independent of national cohort decline.

sources.title: 2025 territorial study of Hungarian settlement demographics and institutional capacity

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian statutory law mandates minimum class size of 26 students (28 recommended, maximum 34) with exceptions only in rare legal cases; as local cohorts fall below these thresholds, forming even single viable gimnázium intake classes becomes numerically untenable, creating mechanical constraint independent of policy preference.

sources.title: Hungarian education law: statutory class-size requirements

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian teacher workforce is aging (50% age 50+, only 7.2% under age 30) with critical shortages in rural and disadvantaged areas (unqualified teacher proportion 2-3 times higher in disadvantaged schools), creating compounding institutional strain on any multi-track selective provision model in peripheral regions independent of cohort decline.

sources.title: Hungarian education sector workforce analysis, 2024/2025 academic year

evidence_appendix.claim: Entry to 6/8-year gimnázium tracks is stratified by socioeconomic background; families must invest substantial financial, social, and cultural capital to compete for places, and elementary schools serving disadvantaged populations often do not facilitate student participation in the entry competition.

sources.title: Longitudinal study of selective grammar school entry processes in Hungary

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian school financing operates on a capitation (per-capita) basis; schools that lose high-performing students to 6/8-year gimnáziums lose both the student's funding and the positive peer effects that high-performing peers provide to remaining students, with no compensatory funding mechanism documented for feeder schools.

sources.title: Hungarian Ministry of Education financing structure and normative per capita contribution rules

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's overall public education spending is relatively low (4.1% of government expenditure, ranking 41/42 in OECD/partner comparison, 2022), and intended instruction time for lower secondary students ranks 32/34 among OECD countries (796 hours, 2023), making internal resource allocation between selective and comprehensive tracks more consequential.

sources.title: OECD Education at a Glance 2024 and Hungarian education statistics

evidence_appendix.claim: Among disadvantaged students in Hungary, only 12.1% reach a good level in at least one basic skill (reading, mathematics, science) in 2022 PISA, considerably below the EU average of 16.3%, with early school leaving rates among Roma at 58.7% versus 9.3% among non-Roma (2023).

sources.title: OECD PISA 2022 cycle and Hungarian Labour Force Survey (2023)

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary selects students into academic tracks at age 10–12, earlier than almost any comparable OECD system, and cross-country panel evidence shows early tracking is significantly associated with higher educational inequality and a tendency toward reduced mean performance.

sources.title: Cross-country panel evidence on tracking and educational inequality

evidence_appendix.claim: Students from higher socioeconomic backgrounds are significantly more likely to access the selective early-academic tracks in Hungary even after controlling for prior ability; these tracks show higher value-added in literacy and tertiary progression, thereby increasing inequality of opportunity.

sources.title: Panel dataset study of selective track access and socioeconomic stratification

evidence_appendix.claim: Entry to selective grammar-school tracks functions as a capital-intensive competition requiring families to invest financial, social, and cultural resources before formal testing occurs; elementary schools serving disadvantaged populations report not offering genuine participation opportunities to pupils.

sources.title: Study of secondary school selection process in Hungarian grammar schools (2018 dissertation)

evidence_appendix.claim: Students remaining in ordinary primary tracks after selection show deteriorating test scores and reduced performance relative to peers in selective tracks, indicating selection relocates rather than concentrates advantage.

sources.title: Horn (2013) and Gurzó & Horn follow-up research on value-added of tracking schools

evidence_appendix.claim: Ethnic Roma segregation in Hungarian schools has significantly increased since 1989 and is distinct from socioeconomic segregation; Roma students are concentrated in weaker tracks system-wide and less likely to attend academic tracks or continue secondary schooling.

sources.title: Kertesi & Kézdi (2011) study of school segregation of Roma children; Roma student track distribution data

evidence_appendix.claim: Segregation and selection in Hungary are amplified by privatization and church-school growth rather than by parental school choice per se; the share of church-run primary schools rose from 8.6% to 16.6% and secondary schools from 10% to 26.1% between 2010 and 2021.

sources.title: OECD cross-country analysis of free school choice and selection; Hungarian church-school enrollment data (2010–2021)

evidence_appendix.claim: Geographic and rural-urban disparities in access to extended-track gimnáziums layer on socioeconomic selection; distance to nearest 6- or 8-year track school is used as a causal instrument because proximity is not randomly distributed and clusters in urban areas.

sources.title: Studies using distance-to-school as instrument for track attendance; Hungarian school location data

evidence_appendix.claim: Even absent formal early-selection tracks, Hungarian schools engage in substantial within-school classroom sorting by socioeconomic status, which has significant effects on test-score inequality; sorting would likely relocate rather than be eliminated if the 6-/8-year gimnázium structure were abolished.

sources.title: Study of within-school sorting using universal 6th- and 8th-grade test-score data

evidence_appendix.claim: Cross-nationally, early between-school tracking at age 10 (as in Hungary, Austria, Germany) is associated with higher educational inequality and greater impact of social background on achievement, compared to comprehensive systems tracking at 15–16 (Finland, Australia, Poland), with no clear equity-efficiency trade-off.

sources.title: OECD comparative tracking and inequality literature; seminal difference-in-differences study and recent PISA-based meta-analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: Finland's comprehensive reform (1960s–70s) replaced a dual-track system with 9 years of untracked basic education to age 16, achieving high mean performance and low between-school variance in early PISA cycles (2000–2009) attributed to egalitarian design.

sources.title: Finnish comprehensive school reform history and early PISA outcomes

evidence_appendix.claim: Finland's PISA mathematics score fell 64 points from 548 (2003) to 484 (2022), and achievement inequality has increased in recent cycles with growing SES achievement gaps despite the formal absence of tracking.

sources.title: PISA 2022 mathematics results and Finnish achievement-gap trend analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: Finland's formally untracked comprehensive system has accommodated informal selection through 'emphasized classes' (since 1990s) using testing and prior achievement, creating within-school segregation by performance level, with class-level performance differentials visible in data.

sources.title: Finnish emphasized classes and selective within-school sorting mechanism

evidence_appendix.claim: School choice patterns in Finland have been associated with growing residential and school segregation through middle-class choice behavior, replicating some selection-like effects within a formally non-tracking system, though scholarly interpretation differs on whether this represents genuine 'erosion' of the comprehensive ideal or resilient adaptation.

sources.title: Finnish school segregation and choice literature; ScienceDirect 2021 study on selective classes and comprehensiveness

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's gimnázium system legally coexists in three parallel track lengths (4-year, 6-year, and 8-year), with the 6- and 8-year tracks introduced in the early 1990s as institutional add-ons to the previously single-structure 8-year basic school system.

sources.title: Eurydice and Hungarian statutory education framework

evidence_appendix.claim: Admission to 6- and 8-grade gimnáziums uses a centrally administered, nationally uniform written examination in mathematics and Hungarian language (45 minutes per subject), with answer keys graded identically nationwide and annual publication by the Ministry of Education.

sources.title: Ministry of Education (Oktatási Hivatal) examination administration

evidence_appendix.claim: Free school choice is a statutory right guaranteed under the Act on Public Education (2011. évi CXC. törvény a nemzeti köznevelésről) and has been explicitly stated as non-negotiable by the current education minister.

sources.title: Act on Public Education (2011) and ministerial statement

evidence_appendix.claim: Early parental-education composition data (circa 2000) shows stark gaps: 43.6% of ninth-graders in 6/8-grade programs had mothers with tertiary education versus 21.3% in the full sample and 9.2% in vocational schools.

sources.title: Hungarian education survey (early 2000s parental education data)

evidence_appendix.claim: A 2019 study cited by the current minister documents that parents preparing children for 8- and 6-grade gimnázium entrance exams invested on average 3.5 hours per week and 34,000 forints per month in exam preparation, with almost exclusive admission of children of university-educated parents.

sources.title: 2019 parental investment study (cited by Minister Lannert)

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary ranks as the highest in the EU for segregation of students from less and more advantaged socioeconomic groups into different schools (Gutiérrez et al., 2020) and shows the highest segregation of most and least disadvantaged students in the 2022 PISA study.

sources.title: Gutiérrez et al. (2020) and OECD PISA 2022

evidence_appendix.claim: A documented case in Encs shows that permission for a school to add lower grades (7-8) to an upper-secondary general school resulted in creaming of higher-status pupils from the final years of two primary schools, demonstrating that 6/8-year track expansion reshapes composition in feeder primary-school systems.

sources.title: Encs case study (local school system reorganization)

evidence_appendix.claim: The current education minister (as of mid-2026) has publicly characterized competency assessment results as showing that 6- and 8-grade gimnázium students achieve better results due to favorable family backgrounds and higher prior knowledge, but progress at the same pace as other schools, framing this as elite self-selection rather than value-added learning.

sources.title: Ministerial statement on national competency assessment (NABC)

evidence_appendix.claim: The Ministry of Education has used discretionary approval power to deny a request by a Reformed Church congregation to introduce an 8-grade gimnázium track at a school in Tiszavasvári, citing concerns that the change would reinforce local school segregation and create irreversible segregated-school situations.

sources.title: Ministry of Education administrative decision on Tiszavasvári school expansion (mid-2026)

evidence_appendix.claim: The current education minister has stated that declining child demographics make the current selective gimnázium system less justifiable and has placed the gymnasium entrance-exam system on the government agenda for review, but has explicitly stated she is not initiating abolition, only review, of the system.

sources.title: Minister Lannert public statements (mid-2026)

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks create selection gates at ages ~10 and ~12 within a system where institutional structures and educational programme structures are not aligned, producing a coordination problem for any reform involving multiple entry points.

sources.title: Hungarian Educational Research Sources on Structural Mechanics

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary shows extreme socio-economic educational inequality, with a 170-point reading comprehension gap between the top and bottom 10% socio-economically—equivalent to four years of formal education—and only 12.1% of disadvantaged students reaching good level in at least one basic skill, considerably below EU average.

sources.title: PISA Analysis and EU 2025 Educational Monitor

evidence_appendix.claim: The European Court of Human Rights has repeatedly ruled against educational segregation of Roma children and ordered Hungary to develop a policy eliminating segregation; as of 2024, the Committee of Ministers monitoring a 2013 judgment has been unable to close the case due to absence of demonstrated elimination of discriminatory practices, and European Commission infringement proceedings launched in 2016 remain without public closure.

sources.title: ECtHR Judgments and EU Infringement Proceedings

evidence_appendix.claim: Church-run institutions now account for approximately 20% of all schools and over 33% of secondary schools in Hungary, with documented patterns of preferential state funding, attraction of higher-qualified teachers, and admissions discretion enabling selection of higher-achieving students, alongside reported exodus of non-Roma children from state schools to church institutions.

sources.title: UN Special Rapporteur Visit Report, March 2025

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary faces a teacher-workforce capacity crisis: enrollment in teacher training has fallen by approximately 25% over four years, creating a shortage of 16,000 teachers, with aging workforce concentration such that half of all teachers in 2024/2025 were age 50 or older and only 7.2% were under 30, limiting redeployment capacity for structural education reform.

sources.title: Hungarian Teacher Workforce Statistics 2024/2025

evidence_appendix.claim: Government and civil-society actors dispute basic statistics on education finance, international and national student performance test results, and indicators of church-sector participation, undermining the existence of a shared, trusted measurement baseline required for independent feedback loops to evaluate educational reforms.

sources.title: Civil Society Monitoring and Government Statistical Discrepancies

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary has one of the highest rates of school-based academic selection in OECD countries: 85% of Hungarian students attend schools that select students by academic performance, compared to the OECD average of 43%.

sources.title: OECD education profile for Hungary

evidence_appendix.claim: Between-school variance in Hungary is the third highest among all OECD countries, while within-school variance is below the OECD average, indicating that early tracking concentrates educational inequality between schools rather than within them.

sources.title: OECD education profile for Hungary

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's socioeconomic achievement gap is among the largest in the OECD: socioeconomic background explains 23.1% of student performance variance, compared to the OECD average of 14.8%.

sources.title: OECD education profile for Hungary

evidence_appendix.claim: Cross-national econometric evidence using TIMSS/PIRLS/PISA (Hanushek & Woessmann) finds that early tracking increases educational inequality and shows a tendency to reduce mean performance, with Hungary named as an early-tracking country.

sources.title: Hanushek & Woessmann (difference-in-differences study on TIMSS/PIRLS/PISA)

evidence_appendix.claim: Micro-level evidence from Bavaria indicates poor predictive validity of age-10 selection: only 4-5% of students in the Gymnasium achieved better results in German or Math than students in the middle school, and approximately half of students deemed ineligible for Gymnasium based on 4th-grade grades had test results matching Gymnasium students.

sources.title: Böheim-Galehr & Engleitner (2014) cited in German education comparisons

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's early academic tracking is structurally entangled with ethnic segregation of Roma children, a dimension absent from tracking debates in Germany, Austria, or Finland: schools with selective early tracks concentrate non-Roma elite students while relegating Roma and socially underprivileged children to less-prestigious classes.

sources.title: European Court of Human Rights decision Horváth and Kiss v. Hungary; EU Commission infringement proceedings (2016); Hungarian education research synthesis (2024)

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 1999 tracking-age delay (from 15 to 16) was bundled with curriculum harmonization and structural reforms, and its 2017 reversal to an 8+4 structure produced acute transition costs: in 2017-18, both gimnazjum and primary-school graduates competed for high-school places simultaneously, creating severe capacity shortfalls (e.g., 3,173 students in Warsaw alone did not secure secondary-school placement).

sources.title: Polish education reform records (1999, 2017); Warsaw municipal education data

evidence_appendix.claim: Finland's comprehensive school reform (1972-1977) increased the tracking-age delay from 11 to 16 and was bundled with ability-grouping abolition (1985) and systemic curriculum/teacher-training changes; long-run evaluations show positive but modest effects on attainment and earnings, with only small improvements in test scores.

sources.title: Finnish education reform records; Pekkarinen, Uusitalo & Kerr study

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's system combines early academic tracking with unrestricted free school choice and student commuting; towns with higher student commuting to chosen schools show stronger inter-school inequalities, suggesting that the selection mechanism in Hungary is not tracking alone but the interaction of tracking with choice—a bundled institutional context not present in the same form in Germany or comprehensive Nordic systems.

sources.title: Hungarian education research on school choice and inter-school inequality

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks are codified in primary legislation (Act CXC of 2011 on National Public Education), meaning any structural change requires parliamentary amendment rather than ministerial decree.

sources.title: Act CXC of 2011 on National Public Education (Nkt.)

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's Fundamental Law guarantees a constitutionally protected right to free choice of educational institution, creating a legal design tension: restricting early-selection tracks without restricting parental choice is not straightforward under current constitutional doctrine.

sources.title: Hungary's Fundamental Law and Public Education Act (CXC/2011) education-rights provisions

evidence_appendix.claim: Student assignment to gimnázium programs nationwide is administered through a centralized deferred-acceptance algorithm (used since 2000) that requires schools to rank applicants according to criteria including grades, exams, and interviews (with interview weight capped at 25% by regulation).

sources.title: Hungarian school placement system and KIFIR national matching infrastructure

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary exhibits the highest level of socioeconomic segregation between schools in the EU (measured by concentration of advantaged vs. disadvantaged students), with a 53-point PISA performance gap between children of graduate and non-graduate parents compared to 33-point EU average.

sources.title: OECD PISA 2015 science assessment and EU Commission monitoring (Gutiérrez et al., 2020); PISA 2022 study

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary operates one of the most selective education systems in the EU: between-school variation in outcomes exceeds that in Austria, Germany, Lithuania, and the Netherlands (all of which also practice age-10/11 early tracking), indicating that Hungary's selection intensity is an outlier even among early-tracking countries.

sources.title: OECD PISA 2015 survey (science assessment); comparative PISA analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: Since 2013, school maintenance authority has been centralized in the Klebelsberg Centre and 60 district sub-units under state control, but this jurisdiction does not extend to schools operated by church, foundation, nationality-government, or purely private entities.

sources.title: Klebelsberg Institution Maintenance Centre governance structure; Eurydice/Hungarian education administration sources

evidence_appendix.claim: The Hungarian education system contains four formal pre-higher-education selection points (entry to early-childhood education, primary school choice, age-10/12 gimnázium entry, and post-16 tracking), meaning reform of the 6/8-year track alone may have limited systemic effect on aggregate segregation without addressing capacity allocation and other junctures.

sources.title: Hungarian education-system selection analysis; research on capacity-gap dynamics in social selection

evidence_appendix.claim: A 2023 law extended uniform state employment terms across all provider types (state, church, private, foundation), making teachers and institutions subject to a unified legal status regime and strengthening school district centres' employer and maintenance powers.

sources.title: 2023 Hungarian teacher employment law; government plans documentation and critical responses from unions and NGOs

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary simultaneously guarantees constitutional protection for institutional and parental educational choice while operating a highly centralized operational system (curriculum, textbooks, staffing centrally controlled), creating a structural governance tension: any reform of the gimnázium entry age would require clarification of which authority locus (Parliament, Klebelsberg Centre, individual maintainers, or church/private entities) controls implementation.

sources.title: Comparative EU constitutional law analysis of education-system autonomy; Hungarian Fundamental Law; Klebelsberg Centre governance documents

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 1999 reform introducing a comprehensive three-year lower-secondary stage (gimnazjum) correlated with PISA gains between 2000–2015 and documented reductions in socio-economic performance gaps, particularly benefiting formerly vocational-track students.

sources.title: NCEE/Springer literature synthesized at worldclasslearningsystems.com; Springer chapter 'Reforming Education in Poland'

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2017 reversal restored an 8-year primary structure and abolished gimnazjum, advancing the first tracking point by one year, with implementation occurring over 10 months rather than through phased transition.

sources.title: Medium/Lambert 2019; euronews.com 2017; Springer chapter on Polish education reform

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2017 reform created acute short-term implementation disruption including a double cohort effect causing 3,173 unplaced secondary students in Warsaw alone, closure of approximately 7,000 junior high schools, and potential job losses for 10,000 teachers.

sources.title: balkaninsight.com/Reporting Democracy 2020; wenr.wes.org 2023

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2018 PISA results showing top European performance and reduced inequality gaps reflect the outgoing gimnazjum cohort that was not exposed to the 8+4 reversed structure, not evidence of the new system's effectiveness.

sources.title: Springer chapter 'Reforming Education in Poland' by Wiśniewski & Zahorska 2020

evidence_appendix.claim: Free primary-school choice in Hungary operates as an independent segregation mechanism from the optional gimnázium tracks, with schools permitted to hand-pick students, creating residential and socio-economic sorting that is analytically distinct from early academic selection.

sources.title: Academia.edu thesis on Hungarian primary-school segregation

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks were introduced in the early 1990s as post-1990 policy additions layered onto an inherited 8-grade comprehensive system, not ancient institutions, which affects feasibility by allowing both selective tracks and comprehensive defaults to be framed as 'status quo' by different actors.

sources.title: Baseline institutional facts on Hungarian education structure

evidence_appendix.claim: Early tracking in Hungary produces stronger selection effects than in three of four European comparison countries (Austria, Germany, Lithuania, Netherlands), with cross-national evidence showing early tracking increases educational inequality, though effects on mean performance are less robust.

sources.title: Hanushek and Woessmann cross-national analysis; PISA 2015 science assessment data

evidence_appendix.claim: Earlier age of selection and number of school-types are significantly associated with higher inequality of opportunity across OECD countries; applied to Hungary, selective tracks cream-skim the best students at ages 10 and 12 rather than the official age 14, and recent administrative data confirm school segregation by social status remains among the highest in Europe.

sources.title: OECD comparative inequality literature; Hungarian administrative national-assessment data (2026 study)

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's education minister (2026) and supporting Hungarian media coverage assert that measured performance differences between selective and comprehensive tracks are primarily explained by family background/selection effects rather than added institutional value, framing the selective system as serving elite self-perpetuation rather than educational improvement.

sources.title: Statements by appointed education minister and Hungarian media coverage of competence-measurement data

evidence_appendix.claim: The 2010–2026 FIDESZ-KDNP coalition government explicitly embraced educational stratification as ideological policy, with a 2012-era human-resources minister stating the government would pursue 'affectionate segregation' on the grounds that lower-income pupils are less educationally able.

sources.title: Statement by FIDESZ minister of human resources; 2012 legislation and centralization decision

evidence_appendix.claim: Municipal-level 'elite capture' dynamics and parental school-choice behavior create self-reinforcing demand for selective tracks among middle-class families, particularly in small towns identified as an important voter base for the Orbán regime.

sources.title: Decade-old analysis of municipal gatekeeping; recent ethnographic research on parental school-choice politics under Orbán

evidence_appendix.claim: Gimnazium directors constitute an organized interest group with demonstrated direct access to top political leadership (17 principals collectively lobbied Prime Minister-designate and received personal meetings), positioning them as a concrete veto player relevant to both enactment and reversal of reform.

sources.title: Collective letter from 17 gimnázium principals to Prime Minister-designate Péter Magyar; documented subsequent personal meetings

evidence_appendix.claim: The ministry administratively rejected a church-run school's request to launch an 8-grade gimnázium on segregation grounds, demonstrating that discretionary administrative tools can constrain selective-track expansion without new legislation, and that disadvantaged-family advocacy groups actively monitor and publicize such decisions.

sources.title: Ministry decision denying Reformed Church congregation of Tiszavasvári's request; statements by deputy ombudsman for national minorities; Roma press center and National Association of Disadvantaged Families monitoring

evidence_appendix.claim: School-system performance differences between Hungary and high-performing comprehensive-system countries like Finland are stark: between-school variation in Hungary is significantly higher than in Finland, and socio-economic background has larger impact on performance in Hungary, indicating the system reproduces rather than reduces social inequalities.

sources.title: OECD comparative analysis of school variation and socioeconomic-impact measures; Finland benchmarking

evidence_appendix.claim: Comparative cases (Finland 1970s, France age-11-to-13 delay, Poland 1999) show delayed tracking improved equity and attainment, but only when bundled with unified national curricula, extended compulsory comprehensive schooling, and coordinated curricular reform—not as isolated interventions

sources.title: Cross-national comprehensive school reform analysis (Finland, France, Poland)

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary adopted free school-choice systems for elementary schools with discretionary pupil selection, creating segregation by school characteristics before formal age-12 gimnázium tracking begins, suggesting structural change to 6/8-year tracks alone may not resolve segregation originating in primary-school choice

sources.title: Central European University policy analysis of Hungarian school-choice segregation mechanisms

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's 2023 teacher-career reform (Act LII) and targeted subject-teacher incentive funding for talent development aim at non-structural quality improvement but have not been evaluated for impact on equity gaps documented in international assessments

sources.title: Act LII of 2023 on New Careers of Teachers and subject-specific grant scheme data

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's partial reversal of its successful 1999 comprehensive reform (delaying vocational tracking) correlates with PISA 2022 performance decline, but the decline is confounded by COVID-19 pandemic learning loss in the same PISA cycle

sources.title: PISA 2022 Poland and post-1999-reform reversal analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian government has selectively invoked PISA equity findings to legitimize reforms but implementation has lagged; PISA reception revealed skepticism and has been mediated by local cultural contexts and institutional disorganization, preventing alignment with OECD-recommended approaches

sources.title: Study of PISA reception and policy translation in Hungarian education governance

evidence_appendix.claim: Roma students face substantially higher early school-leaving rates (58.7% vs. 9.3% non-Roma in 2023) in least-developed regions and villages, indicating ethnic/regional equity gaps distinct from and extending beyond age-12 gimnázium selection structures

sources.title: EU early school-leaving statistics and Hungarian regional/ethnic breakdown (2023-2024)

evidence_appendix.claim: Six- and eight-year gimnázium enrollment represents approximately 8% of each age cohort, with applications to six-year programs rising 11% year-on-year while eight-year applications fell 14% in 2026, suggesting a shift toward the more selective later-entry track.

sources.title: Dániel Horn podcast interview and 2026 Hungarian secondary admission data

evidence_appendix.claim: Physical capacity in six- and eight-year gimnáziums remained stagnant at approximately 25,962 students in 2024/2025 despite extreme oversubscription (flagship schools receiving 9–26 times more applicants than available places), with one education researcher attributing this to policy decisions redirecting students toward vocational training.

sources.title: Eduline reporting on Hungarian gimnázium admissions and researcher commentary

evidence_appendix.claim: School-district authorities recommended entrance-exam score thresholds of 70 points (out of stated scale) for admission to six- and eight-year gymnasia in 2024/25, with parents' advocacy groups arguing this could reduce available places and push students involuntarily into vocational training.

sources.title: Hungarian school-district admission guidance and parents' advocacy group reporting via press

evidence_appendix.claim: Six- and eight-year gimnázium programs are geographically concentrated in large urban settlements and Budapest, with historically established and larger schools retaining greater selective capacity than later-founded or rural schools regardless of track type, indicating path-dependent geographic stratification.

sources.title: Hungarian educational research on school founding dates and selective capacity; settlement-level analysis of 1999/2000 gimnázium distribution

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's secondary education network comprises 880 schools designed to ensure proximity (public transport access within one hour) for all students, with dormitory places available for those unable to commute; however, this proximity guarantee applies to generic secondary schools, not specifically to geographically clustered six- and eight-year selective tracks.

sources.title: Hungarian education system network planning documentation

evidence_appendix.claim: Upper grades (5–8) of ordinary primary schools nationally experience 'dilution' (felhígulás) in teaching staff and peer composition as students depart for gimnázium or higher-status schools, creating excess capacity in feeder schools and staffing complications while concentrating demand on selective urban institutions.

sources.title: Hungarian education research on primary-school attrition and network effects; settlement-level case examples

evidence_appendix.claim: Rural secondary schools face infrastructure fragility and closure risk, with potential settlement-viability consequences if the local secondary institution closes partly due to student outflow toward selective tracks, though this evidence derives from a single interview and has not been systematically audited.

sources.title: Válasz Online interview with gimnázium director, June 2026

content.generation_notice: Fresh live v2 production replicate using the admitted lens registry. Generator=anthropic; judge=openai; human external-use gate remains pending.
content.public_question: Should Hungary keep its 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks?
content.summary:
Hungary's 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks sit inside a system already ranked highest in the EU for socioeconomic school segregation [F-live-hungarian_education_system-06], produced by early tracking [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01], its interaction with free school choice [F-live-international_comparison-09], and a coaching market concentrated among university-educated parents [F-live-hungarian_education_system-05]. Six directions define the option space, distinguished by how much of the existing legal-institutional architecture they leave untouched versus restructure.

T1 (coaching-resistant SES-weighted admission redesign) and T2 (state-sector capacity contraction via thresholds) both work entirely inside the existing exam and ministerial-discretion infrastructure [F-live-hungarian_education_system-02, F-live-legal_and_governance-03, F-live-hungarian_education_system-09], requiring no new legislation. Their shared constraint is jurisdictional and mechanistic partiality: neither reaches non-applicant pools in disadvantaged feeder schools [F-live-education_finance-02], neither touches free choice or primary-school sorting that independently drives segregation [F-live-portuguese_reform-06, F-live-polish_reform-07], and T2's contraction is legally confined to the Klebelsberg Centre, excluding church/private schools that are the fastest-growing selective segment [F-live-legal_and_governance-07, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07]. T6 closes that maintainer gap by extending precedented discretionary gatekeeping (already used in the Tiszavasvári refusal [F-live-hungarian_education_system-09] and demonstrated causally in the Encs creaming case [F-live-hungarian_education_system-07]) across all providers, but remains preventive only, unable to remediate already-existing segregated tracks.

T3 (statutory delay of tracking to 14+ with a unified comprehensive phase) has the strongest comparative rationale: cross-national evidence links early tracking to higher inequality and flat-to-lower mean performance [F-live-international_comparison-04], and Finland's 1972-77 reform delayed tracking from 11 to 16 [F-live-international_comparison-08]. But that evidence also establishes a value-relevant condition, not just an empirical one: delay worked only bundled with ability-grouping abolition and curriculum/teacher-training reform [F-live-finnish_reform-06, F-live-portuguese_reform-05]. Empirically, Poland's 1999 delay correlated with equity gains for former vocational-track students, while its unphased 2017 reversal caused 3,173 unplaced students in Warsaw [F-live-polish_reform-03, F-live-polish_reform-05]—a direct transition-design warning. T3's material constraints are legal (primary legislation plus a constitutionally protected free-choice right [F-live-legal_and_governance-01, F-live-legal_and_governance-02]) and capacity-based (a 16,000-teacher shortfall and aging workforce [F-live-implementation_planning-07]).

T4 (compensatory funding, unchanged selection age) targets a concrete resource asymmetry—capitation funding strips feeder schools of both revenue and peer effects with no offset [F-live-education_finance-05]—and Hungary's low disadvantaged-skill attainment (12.1% vs 16.3% EU average) leaves room for such investment [F-live-education_finance-07]. Its constraint is that comparative cases show non-structural measures worked only as a complement to, not substitute for, structural delay [F-live-portuguese_reform-05], and within-school SES sorting would likely persist regardless [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10].

T5 (passive demographic-contraction baseline) is not a policy but a counterfactual: birth cohorts are collapsing structurally for 20+ years [F-live-demography-01, F-live-demography-02], statutory class-size minimums (26-34) make some rural intake classes numerically untenable [F-live-demography-05], and passive shifts are already visible in application data [F-live-school_network_planning-02]. Its risk, empirically grounded, is geographic unevenness—urban capacity may persist while rural provision collapses [F-live-demography-03, F-live-demography-04]—leaving Roma segregation and church-sector growth wholly unaddressed [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06, F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07].

The empirical uncertainties concentrate on displacement: whether coaching advantage migrates to redesigned instruments (as Finland's 'emphasized classes' suggest [F-live-finnish_reform-08]), whether contracted state capacity is absorbed by exempt maintainers, and whether Hungary's specific entanglement of tracking with ethnic segregation and free choice [F-live-international_comparison-06, F-live-international_comparison-09] limits transferability of Finnish/Polish comparators. The value choices are separable: whether to accept a lower, more certain equity ceiling for legal/political feasibility (T1, T2, T4, T6) versus pursue larger, contested structural change (T3) against an ideologically committed governing coalition and an organized director lobby with direct political access [F-live-political_feasibility-05, F-live-political_feasibility-07]; and whether free choice should be treated as a fixed constraint or a legitimate reform target. Research that would most change the choice: the causal contribution of track structure itself net of choice and church expansion; where displaced demand and advantage would relocate under each lever; and whether a phased, resourced delay is feasible given the teacher shortage [F-live-implementation_planning-07].
content.title: Early academic selection and the 6/8-year gimnázium
